    "Raw_Text": "Return to the incident list:\nIncident List\nI attempted to drag the inside wheel during turn at ground level.  The sand dune was coming up and I attempted to match its angle with my bank angle smooth enough to drag the lower inside wheel during the turn.  I misjudged my approach velocity, didn\u2019t grease it on a smooth as I should have and accidentally drug my front wheel as well.  It quickly bent back so far that it hit my leg.  It broke a bolt that held it on, but still had another so it didn\u2019t fall off.  I bent it back into place and continued flying.  Luckily I got it hard enough to still have enough momentum to keep the wing flying with a few pumps of the brakes.  :)  upon landing an hour later, the front wheel fork quickly started to collapse under my weight, so I put both feet down and skidded to a halt like Fred Flintstone, with the entire trike hanging from my back.  When I stopped completely, I sat down on a broken trike an unhooked from my harness.  :)\nPPG Type\n: Wheel Launch \u2013 Single Occupant\nType of Injury\n: Minor Injury\nPilot Details\nAge\n: 47\nWeight\n: 215\nGender\n: Male\nHighest rating held at the time of the incident\n: Advanced \u2013 PPG 3\nPilot experience level\n: 50+ hours\nGear Details\nWing Brand\n: Dudek\nModel\n: Snake\nSize\n: 20\nParamotor Frame\n: PAP Tinox with Polini Thor 260\nIncident Details\nFebruary 11, 2025 5:00 PM Location of the incident:\nGila Bend, Arizona\nType of Incident\n: Collision with terrain or ground obstacle, Pilot Error\nI had previously been flying a wing that was much more maneuverable at slow speeds than the snake. \u00a0I misjudged the speed because of that. \u00a0When changing wings regularly, one must take into account the differences in their flying characteristics or this stuff can happen. \u00a0I was very lucky.\nFlight Window\n: Evening\nWind Speed\n: Moderate Wind (5-9 mph)\nType\n: Light and Variable\nPhase of Flight\n: Cruise/In Flight\nType of Injury\n: Minor Injury\nCollateral Damage\n:\nAnalysis of the incident (additional input by the incident investigation team)\n:\nPhotos\n(if available):\nVideo\n(if available): https://www.instagram.com/reel/DF6B2UrTCMU/?igsh=MTJkeTB1Y3NwYTl0dg==\nOther Files\n(if available):\nReturn to the incident list:\nIncident List",

    "Raw_Text": "Return to the incident list:\nIncident List\nUnintended ground impact due to altitude misjudgment\nPPG Type\n: Foot Launch \u2013 Single Occupant\nType of Injury\n: Major Injury\nPilot Details\nAge\n:\nWeight\n:\nGender\n:\nHighest rating held at the time of the incident\n: Instructor\nPilot experience level\n: 50+ hours\nGear Details\nWing Brand\n: Ozone\nModel\n:\nSize\n:\nParamotor Frame\n:   with Vittorazi Moster 185\nIncident Details\nNovember 19, 2025 8:00 AM Location of the incident:\nOther, Other\nType of Incident\n: Collision with terrain or ground obstacle\nThe pilot was flying over desert terrain with small sand dunes. The natural lack of visual markings on the ground in this terrain along with variability in the ground level due to the sand dunes interfered with the pilot\u2019s ability to judge their altitude. While unintentionally flying at a low altitude, the pilot impacted the ground upon executing a turn.\nNo other factors were identified in this accident. Weather conditions were optimal and there was no equipment failure.\nThe main lesson is to understand that, similar to flying over water, flying over uniform-colored sand can interfere with depth perception. It is important to ensure plenty of altitude over such terrains for safety.\nFlight Window\n: Morning\nWind Speed\n: Moderate Wind (5-9 mph)\nType\n: Steady / Laminar\nPhase of Flight\n: Cruise/In Flight\nType of Injury\n: Major Injury\nCollateral Damage\n: None\nAnalysis of the incident (additional input by the incident investigation team)\n:\nPhotos\n(if available):\nVideo\n(if available):\nOther Files\n(if available):\nReturn to the incident list:\nIncident List",

    "Raw_Text": "Return to the incident list:\nIncident List\nI was performing a SAT maneuver when an outer central A line broke at the cascade, causing the majority of my lines to then break until my glider balled up and I was required to throw my reserve.\nPPG Type\n: Foot Launch \u2013 Single Occupant\nType of Injury\n: No Injury\nPilot Details\nAge\n: 26\nWeight\n: 129\nGender\n: Female\nHighest rating held at the time of the incident\n: Advanced \u2013 PPG 3\nPilot experience level\n: 50+ hours\nGear Details\nWing Brand\n: Davinci\nModel\n: Disco\nSize\n: 16\nParamotor Frame\n: PAP TINOX V2 with Vittorazi Moster 185\nIncident Details\nJanuary 29, 2026 4:45 PM Location of the incident:\nSt. Peterburg, Florida\nType of Incident\n: Wing Malfunction\nWhat happened\nA couple evenings ago, I drove out to Lake Wales to take advantage of good winds before the weekend would be blown out. Setup and preflight were normal \u2014 everything visually checked out. Forward inflation went well and I began to climb up to practice a SAT or two, as I hadn\u2019t done one in awhile.\nI climbed to just over 5,000 ft and entered the SAT as I normally do. Almost immediately, one of my center A lines popped, and it quickly cascaded across into a major equipment failure. Within seconds, I had no glider left. After looking up at the glider and seeing the issue, I threw my rescue. My rescue opened and it took approximately 4 minutes and 24 seconds until I landed out in a field a mile from my launch point. I sustained no major injuries, only minor bruises and scrapes. I was able to walk my gear back to the LZ as a vehicle could not get into the cow pasture I landed in due to gate locks.\nAbout the equipment\nMy glider was a little over a year old with ~45 hours on it. I\u2019m lightly loaded on my glider. I thought I had a bit more time until sending in for inspection \u2014 I didn\u2019t.\nAltitude matters\nAltitude was my biggest ally that day. I\u2019ve been told many times to \u201cdo maneuvers lower\u201d because \u201cit looks cool.\u201d I start high and I finish high \u2014 that\u2019s what I\u2019m comfortable with.\nFailures happen fast. Decisions take time. Reserves take time to open. Risking that just to look cool isn\u2019t worth it. Use a camera. Share it later.\nReserves & risk\nI know pilots who fly without reserves. I flew with two \u2014 and I was lucky the first one opened.\nEvery flight carries risk. Some of us \u2014 myself included \u2014 increase that risk by flying beyond straight and level. It only makes sense to compensate with altitude, training, and proper safety equipment.\nFlight Window\n: Evening\nWind Speed\n: Light Wind (1-5 mph)\nType\n: Light and Variable\nPhase of Flight\n: Cruise/In Flight\nType of Injury\n: No Injury\nCollateral Damage\n: None\nAnalysis of the incident (additional input by the incident investigation team)\n:\nPhotos\n(if available): https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6MTk3ODR8ZmlsZW5hbWU6U25hcHNob3RfMjAyNjAxMjlfMjEwMTAzLXNjYWxlZC5qcGd8c2l6ZTp0aHVtYm5haWw=, https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6MTk3ODV8ZmlsZW5hbWU6U25hcHNob3RfMjAyNjAxMjlfMjEwMTIzLXNjYWxlZC5qcGd8c2l6ZTp0aHVtYm5haWw=, https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6MTk3ODZ8ZmlsZW5hbWU6U25hcHNob3RfMjAyNjAxMjlfMjEwMTM5LXNjYWxlZC5qcGd8c2l6ZTp0aHVtYm5haWw=, https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6MTk3ODd8ZmlsZW5hbWU6U25hcHNob3RfMjAyNjAxMjlfMjEwMTQ1LXNjYWxlZC5qcGd8c2l6ZTp0aHVtYm5haWw=, https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6MTk3ODh8ZmlsZW5hbWU6U25hcHNob3RfMjAyNjAxMjlfMjEwMTYyLXNjYWxlZC5qcGd8c2l6ZTp0aHVtYm5haWw=, https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6MTk3ODl8ZmlsZW5hbWU6U25hcHNob3RfMjAyNjAxMjlfMjEwMTY3LXNjYWxlZC5qcGd8c2l6ZTp0aHVtYm5haWw=, https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6MTk4MTN8ZmlsZW5hbWU6Z2xpZGVyaG91cnMuanBnfHNpemU6dGh1bWJuYWls\nVideo\n(if available): https://www.youtube.com/shorts/WgDyVvhfd_I\nOther Files\n(if available):\nReturn to the incident list:\nIncident List",

While fully accelerated (full trim & speedbar) the pilot reached a ground speed of 48 miles per hour. There was a tension knot in the upper cascade of one of the unsheathed lines which resulted in deformation of the glider. This deformation resulted in an asymmetric collapse & the pilot crashing into the ground. The pilot was flying at a low altitude & focused on creating a video. The pilot had minimal time to react. His hands were not on the brakes. The glider manual does not allow the brakes to be used after a certain point of trim / speedbar. \u00a0The pilot sustained severe injuries which resulted in numerous broken bones, hospitalization, & surgeries. The pilot was able to use Siri to call 911. A bystander was on scene relatively quickly too. The friend landed his paraglider in an open grassy field by North Central Speedway. When the Brainerd man came in to land, it appears there was a mechanical malfunction.\nOn Wednesday night, Crow Wing County Sheriff Eric Klang said in the preliminary assessment it appears something fell off the back of the engine and broke the blade, causing the paraglider to crash into the trees. The paraglider was hung up in a tree. Klang said it appears the man tried to unbuckle himself from the suspended wreck when he fell perhaps as far as 50 feet.\nLifesaving efforts were taken with emergency responders, but the man died at the scene. The crash was before 7 p.m.\n\u201cWe\u2019ll be doing a thorough investigation and trying to gather as much information as we can on it,\u201d Klang said.\nKevin Jones and Jason Paige were working on the bleachers at the motor speedway, just off Business Highway 371 South with Cliff Sasker, who owns the speedway. The paragliders are a common sight on a nice summer night. Sasker said they use the grassy area to take off and land and as a flight area because it\u2019s free of power lines.\nAfter the crash, the three men watched from a distance as the North Memorial Health Air Care helicopter took off from the field without a patient. They watched the life-saving efforts come to a stop and knew the man couldn\u2019t be saved.\nThey saw the two men flying earlier.\n\u201cI don\u2019t even pay these guys any attention,\u201d Paige said, \u201cbut when I \u2014 that\u2019s why I even looked up when he circled back, it\u2019s because I heard that.\u201d\n\u201cSputter,\u201d Sasker added. \u201cHe heard it sputtering.\u201d\n\u201cIt popped and it was stalling on him,\u201d Paige said. \u201cI heard the noise and figured, OK he\u2019s going to come in for a landing, something\u2019s not sounding right.\u201d\nSasker said he came around the corner, saw the chute draped in the tree, then told Jones and Paige. When they realized what happened, they could already hear the ambulance and sirens coming to the scene.\nThe fire department, Sheriff\u2019s Office and North Memorial Health Ambulance and Air Care assisted at the scene.\nFlight Window\n: Evening\nWind Speed\n: N/A or Unknown\nType\n: N/A or Unknown\nPhase of Flight\n: Cruise/In Flight\nType of Injury\n: Fatality\nCollateral Damage\n:\nAnalysis of the incident (additional input by the incident investigation team)\n:\nPhotos\n(if available):\nVideo\n(if available):\nOther Files\n(if available):\nReturn to the incident list:\nIncident List",

The content has been edited to preserve pilot anonymity.\n\u201cOn Tuesday, February 18 at 4:30pm, our dear friend, , suffered a fatal crash on his paramotor at the Salton Sea. He was 32 years old.\nHe was an extremely talented paraglider and paramotor pilot who loved pushing the boundaries of our sport. He competed in acro competitions in Europe, worked at SIV clinics constantly, invented new tricks and stunts, and regularly impressed us all with his skills. He is famous for flying a Beamer 3 reserve under power on his paramotor and also for performing a triple rocket loop.\nOn Tuesday, he was working on a new trick that blended a classic paragliding maneuver (a \u201cjoker\u201d) with the power of a paramotor. He had flown up high, away from view to work on it, and perfect it. It was a beautiful afternoon with classic Salton Sea weather. All his closest friends and other Salton visitors were sitting out on the flight line enjoying each other\u2019s company and watching the paramotors.\nHe then flew over the LZ to display the trick for the flight line at far too low an altitude for our comfort. He pulled off several with perfection. But on the final one, he unloaded the glider too much, collapsed, caught & cut lines in his prop, spiraled to the ground, and perished. There seemingly was a split second where throwing a reserve might have saved him, but he didn\u2019t appear to try. As an expert at recovering from failed acro tricks, we believe that was his only instinct. We were on him within seconds and performed lifesaving actions until paramedics arrived, but he was already gone.\nHe was our friend, our family, our light, and our energy. He was joyful, adventurous, inclusive, ambitious, and giving. We loved him dearly and will miss him terribly.\nIf you would like to help his family with their travel and preparations for his memorial, there is a GoFundMe link below.\u201d\nFlight Window\n: Evening\nWind Speed\n: N/A or Unknown\nType\n: N/A or Unknown\nPhase of Flight\n: Cruise/In Flight\nType of Injury\n: Fatality\nCollateral Damage\n:\nAnalysis of the incident (additional input by the incident investigation team)\n:\nPhotos\n(if available):\nVideo\n(if available):\nOther Files\n(if available):\nReturn to the incident list:\nIncident List",

    "Raw_Text": "Return to the incident list:\nIncident List\nAborted takeoff from 20ft AGL, hard landing resulted in knee injury\nPPG Type\n: Foot Launch \u2013 Single Occupant\nType of Injury\n: Major Injury\nPilot Details\nAge\n: 29\nWeight\n: 201\nGender\n: Male\nHighest rating held at the time of the incident\n: Intermediate \u2013 PPG 2\nPilot experience level\n: Less than 10 hours\nGear Details\nWing Brand\n: BGD\nModel\n: Magic\nSize\n: ML\nParamotor Frame\n: Power2Fly Evo 8 with Vittorazi Moster 185 MY19\nIncident Details\nOctober 11, 2025 7:21 PM Location of the incident:\nPlainwell, Michigan\nType of Incident\n: Collision with terrain or ground obstacle, Hard Landing, Pilot Error\nFoot launch PPG was set up in empty soccer fields. Pre-flight inspection of wing and motor showed no issues. Upon takeoff, pilot observed trajectory was aimed at trees, but there was still enough space in front to immediately land on flat ground. Pilot immediately released throttle to idle and began descent\u2013 witnesses report descent began at approximately 20 feet AGL. Pilot flared at approximately 5 ft AGL per usual flight procedure, descent did not slow, no observed response from wing upon flare. Upon hard landing, pilot attempted to remain on feet instead of performing PLF (parachute landing fall), resulting in ruptured patellar tendon in both knees. Pilot was immediately removed from equipment and driven to nearest hospital by witnesses. Despite falling after hard landing, equipment sustained no observable damage.\nLessons learned:\n1. Takeoff distance was misjudged. Parking at soccer fields was on the north side of the property, as was the tree line (see map). Wing was set up at less than half of the available takeoff distance to avoid walking long distances between parking and wing.\n2. Unfamiliar LZ. Pilot has taken off from this location only once before, taking off into West winds. Incident occurred while taking off into light north winds. There was no other pilot familiar with the area present during incident.\n3. Ascent rate was overestimated due to inexperience. Logged GPS and altimerer data from a previous flight showed a quicker ascent rate due to higher winds. Incident occurred on a day with light to no wind.\n4. Sudden power drop to idle caused surge in wing. Wing surged in front of pilot enough to cause increased descent rate. Wing was unresponsive to flare due to surge.\n5. Pressure on brake toggles during takeoff was released upon sudden power drop, further increasing surge noted in item 4.\n6. Failed to perform PLF upon hard landing. Pilot attempted to stay on feet during landing, resulting in injury. Though knees were bent to absorb impact, enough force was exerted in attempt to remain standing to rupture patellar tendons in both knees.\nFlight Window\n: Evening\nWind Speed\n: Light Wind (1-5 mph)\nType\n: Light and Variable\nPhase of Flight\n: Takeoff\nType of Injury\n: Major Injury\nCollateral Damage\n: None\nAnalysis of the incident (additional input by the incident investigation team)\n:\nPhotos\n(if available): https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6MTc4NTJ8ZmlsZW5hbWU6MTAwMDAwMTIxNC5qcGd8c2l6ZTp0aHVtYm5haWw=\nVideo\n(if available):\nOther Files\n(if available):\nReturn to the incident list:\nIncident List",

    "Raw_Text": "Return to the incident list:\nIncident List\nFlew over congested area to avoid chinook propwash\nPPG Type\n: Foot Launch \u2013 Single Occupant\nType of Injury\n: No Injury\nPilot Details\nAge\n: 31\nWeight\n: 180\nGender\n: Male\nHighest rating held at the time of the incident\n: Instructor\nPilot experience level\n: 50+ hours\nGear Details\nWing Brand\n: BGD\nModel\n: Luna 3\nSize\n: 20\nParamotor Frame\n: Parajet Maverick with Vittorazi Moster 185\nIncident Details\nSeptember 26, 2025 6:49 PM Location of the incident:\nBrighton, Michigan\nType of Incident\n: Close Call, Potential regulation violation\nOn the evening of September 26, a friend & I went for a powered paraglider flight. My friend\u2019s son was playing at a high school football game. We flew our powered paragliders near the game. We avoided flying over congested areas & open assemblies of people, per Part 103 regulations. I flew briefly 1,000ft+ over the lake adjacent to the high school, then departed north west. Unbeknownst to us, the US Army had scheduled a military flyover during the national anthem. The flight tracking app I use has audio cues for nearby air traffic with ads-b out. I was informed of a helicopter to the south. Shortly after this notification, I began looking for the traffic. I saw a chinook south of me, heading west. I took some pictures on my phone, because I thought that was very cool to see. Then the chinook changed heading directly towards me. Paragliders are not rigid wings, & susceptible to collapses from turbulence, especially the violent prop wash of a chinook. I immediately began aggressive maneuvers to lose altitude & quickly scouted a place to land if needed. Powered paragliders can safely & reliably land in a tennis court sized area. I also turned on my smoke system to make myself more visible to the helicopter. Mineral oil is injected into the hot exhaust gasses & creates smoke behind me. I fly around 25mph air speed & was quickly in close proximity to the much faster helicopter. I also descended 750 feet to an altitude of 200ft agl. At our closest point, we were 1/3 mile apart horizontally & 200ft of altitude. The helicopter passed me to the west, then north, & continued a north east heading. This pass was up-wind of me, & the resulting turbulent air was coming my way. I made the decision to climb & fly downwind, away from the potentially dangerous air. My flight path put me over a populated area of a town, roughly 7 city blocks of single family homes. I expect this flight path could be considered a violation of part 103; however, I wanted to avoid the potentially dangerous air that could result in an unrecoverable paraglider. My powered paraglider is equipped with a reserve parachute as a last resort. I do my best to fly safe equipment, in safe weather, & with a safe flying style. I do my best to abide by part 103 regulations. Unfortunately, this was a situation that I don\u2019t think I could have avoided being in. On the rest of the flight, I monitored the chinook on flightradar24. I was flying 50 miles away from the local military base & will think of military flyovers more while flying during football season. My friend had more clearance from the helicopter & no issues. I think a more accessible & affordable ads-b out-like device would be fantastic for ultralights, balloons, vintage aircraft, etc. See & avoid is a principle that is critical to the safety of all, especially as commercial drones begin to operate in the airspace.\nThis was also submitted to NASA ASRS\nFlight Window\n: Evening\nWind Speed\n: Moderate Wind (5-9 mph)\nType\n: Steady / Laminar\nPhase of Flight\n: Cruise/In Flight\nType of Injury\n: No Injury\nCollateral Damage\n: None\nAnalysis of the incident (additional input by the incident investigation team)\n:\nPhotos\n(if available):\nVideo\n(if available):\nOther Files\n(if available):\nReturn to the incident list:\nIncident List",

    "Raw_Text": "Return to the incident list:\nIncident List\nPlastic harness buckle broke, harness strap went into the prop\nPPG Type\n: Foot Launch \u2013 Single Occupant\nType of Injury\n: No Injury\nPilot Details\nAge\n: 43\nWeight\n: 210\nGender\n: Male\nHighest rating held at the time of the incident\n: Intermediate \u2013 PPG 2\nPilot experience level\n: 10-50 hours\nGear Details\nWing Brand\n:\nModel\n:\nSize\n:\nParamotor Frame\n: Parajet Maverick with Moster 185\nIncident Details\nAugust 3, 2025 8:30 AM Location of the incident:\nJersey Shore, Pennsylvania\nType of Incident\n: Prop Strike\nLast year I adjusted the straps on my harness to make the motor sit higher on my back. That increased the length of loose ends of both bottom harness straps, but I checked that they\u2019re short enough and do not interfere with the prop.\nThis year, I replaced my E-Prop with Vitto Fast Acceleration prop and removed the prop spacer. It didn\u2019t cross my mind to check whether the loose ends of the harness straps are now long enough to catch the prop.\nThe incident flight was flight #4 after the prop was replaced. Flights 1-3 before that didn\u2019t uncover the issue as loose ends were wrapped around the straps and were not \u201cdangling\u201d. During the incident flight, during climb out, at about 40-50\u2032, I heard something catching the prop and felt excessive vibration. Initially I thought the part of my windbreaker jacket got sucked into the prop, so I decided to do the \u201cimpossible turn\u201d and landed safely on the field.\nAfter landing, I discovered the starboard lower strap of the harness loose, the plastic buckle missing and about an inch of the strap\u2019s end showing damage from the contact with prop (\u201cfluffy\u201d). The trailing(!) edge on one of the prop blades had a damaged area of about 2\u00d70.5\u2033.\nFrom the video recording its not clear whether the strap buckle broke first and that allowed the strap to go into prop, or the loose end of strap was able to reach the prop first and that broke the buckle. I believe I did not see the straps end being loose during preflight check. That said, after checking the length of loose end of the port side harness strap (was still wrapped around the strap), it was determined it is long enough to catch the prop.\nBottom line:\n1. Add an extra buckle to the bottom straps to keep loose ends secured. Wrapping them around is not a solution. Also consider other methods to secure loose ends especially if you\u2019re not that tall.\n2. Any change in configuration (like replacement of one prop with another model) should be considered a high risk and the whole frame should be checked for any possible loose elements that could reach the prop.\nFlight Window\n: Morning\nWind Speed\n: Light Wind (1-5 mph)\nType\n: Light and Variable\nPhase of Flight\n: Takeoff\nType of Injury\n: No Injury\nCollateral Damage\n: None\nAnalysis of the incident (additional input by the incident investigation team)\n:\nPhotos\n(if available):\nVideo\n(if available):\nOther Files\n(if available):\nReturn to the incident list:\nIncident List",

    "Raw_Text": "Return to the incident list:\nIncident List\nFatal injury sustained when pilot flew into lee side rotor near high mountain peak.\nPPG Type\n: Foot Launch \u2013 Single Occupant\nType of Injury\n: Fatality\nPilot Details\nAge\n: 25\nWeight\n:\nGender\n: Male\nHighest rating held at the time of the incident\n: Intermediate \u2013 PPG 2\nPilot experience level\n: 10-50 hours\nGear Details\nWing Brand\n: Ozone\nModel\n: Roadster 3\nSize\n: Unknown\nParamotor Frame\n: Macfly Travel Frame with Vittorazi Moster 185\nIncident Details\nOctober 25, 2024 6:00 PM Location of the incident:\nWillard, Utah\nType of Incident\n: Collision with terrain or ground obstacle\nPilot took off from the small town of Willard, Utah on Friday evening around 5:45pm. Reported winds on the ground were rather light in comparison to the area of the incident. 4-8 mph out of the South-Southwest.\nPilot immediately started to climb towards the mountains to the east. The mountains in question are quite unforgiving. Massive, shear cliffs with deep pocket canyons. All of it impossible to land on let alone hike to it easily. Flight only lasted 22 minutes but he spent most of the time climbing the 5000 feet towards the highest peak (Willard Peak). He climbed steadily along the range maintaining a decent distance from them. \u00a0The general direction the range runs is NW-SE. The reported winds at the neighboring peak 10 miles to the SE were saying 16-23 mph winds from the S-SE. I suspect the winds for him on climb out were fairly smooth but strong because that area would be exposed to wind directly.\nAs he neared Willard Peak, but still below it in terms of elevation, he turned to the north again for a couple minutes before turning back towards the south for the final time. When flying south, he approached Willard Peak a lot closer. The geography of Willard Peak is not a standard peak, there is a false peak that sits directly to the south of it but slightly lower. With that wind direction from the south, it would have created a pocket of lee side rotor that was previously unnoticeable when he was lower and farther out. As he approached and passed Willard Peak, it appears he hit this rotor aggressively and likely caused the wing to be unflyable. The pilot was at an altitude of roughly 50-100 feet from the nearest point of the ground. He plummeted quickly to the ground with no visible signs of a reserve toss. He struck the ground about 100 ft shy of the peak on the southwest side. I would assume there was some sort of line over or the prop potentially struck the lines of the glider in the extreme turbulence. Unfortunately the pilot was suspected of carrying a reserve due to seeing recent social media posts which showed a reserve was attached. It is unknown if there was an attempt to throw the reserve at all but the video does not seem to show it.\nA lot of this information was obtained through Gaggle. Which is exactly how the family was able to notify SAR about a potential crash. After Gaggle notified his emergency contact, they were unable to reach him so they SAR was sent up to the last point the pilot was on the flight track. When they arrived to incident by foot, it was late and the pilot had already perished. It is unknown if he was dead on impact or not. After the next day, a helicopter was sent up to recover the body and gear.\nBy chance, there happened to be a couple on a drive up there and they had dash cam footage of the incident in real time. It\u2019s far away but you can clearly see a \u201csteady\u201d aircraft drop instantly.\nI\u2019ve included screenshots of Gaggle flight tracks, google earth screenshots, 3D flight track recordings, and real photos of the area. I also included the dash cam footage of the crash. I am unaware if he was wearing a GoPro at the time either.\nFlight Window\n: Evening\nWind Speed\n: Strong Wind (10-15 mph)\nType\n: Gusty / Thermal Activity / Dust Devil\nPhase of Flight\n: Cruise/In Flight\nType of Injury\n: Fatality\nCollateral Damage\n:\nAnalysis of the incident (additional input by the incident investigation team)\n:\nPhotos\n(if available): https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6MTM1NTV8ZmlsZW5hbWU6SU1HXzQ3MjQucG5nfHNpemU6dGh1bWJuYWls, https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6MTM1NTZ8ZmlsZW5hbWU6SU1HXzYxNTcuanBlZ3xzaXplOnRodW1ibmFpbA==, https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6MTM1NTd8ZmlsZW5hbWU6SU1HXzQ4MjQucG5nfHNpemU6dGh1bWJuYWls, https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6MTM1NTh8ZmlsZW5hbWU6SU1HXzQ4MjMucG5nfHNpemU6dGh1bWJuYWls, https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6MTM1NTl8ZmlsZW5hbWU6SU1HXzQ4MzkuanBlZ3xzaXplOnRodW1ibmFpbA==\nVideo\n(if available):\nOther Files\n(if available): https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6MTM1NjB8ZmlsZW5hbWU6U2NyZWVuUmVjb3JkaW5nXzEwLTI2LTIwMjQtMTMtMTgtMTFfMS5tb3Y=, https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6MTM1NjN8ZmlsZW5hbWU6NjU0ODYxOTUtRDczMi00MkM1LTg0OTItNDc4OEQ5MTYwMjY1LTEubW92\nReturn to the incident list:\nIncident List",

    "Raw_Text": "Return to the incident list:\nIncident List\nPilot sustains severe injuries while ground-starting.\nPPG Type\n: Foot Launch \u2013 Single Occupant\nType of Injury\n: Major Injury\nPilot Details\nAge\n: 60\nWeight\n: 185\nGender\n: Male\nHighest rating held at the time of the incident\n: Advanced \u2013 PPG 3\nPilot experience level\n: 50+ hours\nGear Details\nWing Brand\n:\nModel\n:\nSize\n:\nParamotor Frame\n:   with\nIncident Details\nOctober 7, 2023 8:30 AM Location of the incident:\nBarceloneta, Puerto Rico\nType of Incident\n: Prop Strike, Pilot Error\nOn Saturday, October 7th, 2023, a severe accident took place in Barceloneta, Puerto Rico, where a veteran paramotor pilot suffered significant injuries while attempting a ground-start.\nAs is common practice among the Puerto Rican paramotoring community, the pilot ground-started his paramotor. However, the throttle was inadvertently pressed against the ground, causing an unexpected surge in power. This sudden thrust propelled his paramotor forward, causing his hands to come into contact with the propeller, resulting in a severed right thumb tip and a deep gash on his left hand. The impact shattered the propeller, and a fragment struck his chest, fortunately shielded by his phone which was stowed on his chest harness. This debris could have easily hit critical areas like his face or neck. The future functionality of his left thumb remains uncertain due to the depth of the injury.\nAlarmingly, many in the local paramotoring community continue to downplay the dangers of ground-starting, often emphasizing details like the throttle\u2019s position. However, the inherent risks of ground-starting cannot be overstated. I believe that this lack awareness is due to the fact that they are taught to ground-start when first learning the sport and this practice quickly becomes normalized.\nFor safety\u2019s sake, NEVER ground-start your paramotor, as it\u2019s a predominant cause of injuries in the sport.\nFlight Window\n: Morning\nWind Speed\n: N/A or Unknown\nType\n: N/A or Unknown\nPhase of Flight\n: Run-up\nType of Injury\n: Major Injury\nCollateral Damage\n: None\nAnalysis of the incident (additional input by the incident investigation team)\n:\nPhotos\n(if available):\nVideo\n(if available):\nOther Files\n(if available):\nReturn to the incident list:\nIncident List",

    "Raw_Text": "Return to the incident list:\nIncident List\nPilot sustains serious arm injury while ground starting\nPPG Type\n: Foot Launch \u2013 Single Occupant\nType of Injury\n: Major Injury\nPilot Details\nAge\n: 72\nWeight\n: 185\nGender\n: Male\nHighest rating held at the time of the incident\n: None\nPilot experience level\n: 50+ hours\nGear Details\nWing Brand\n: Fresh Breeze\nModel\n: N/A\nSize\n: N/A\nParamotor Frame\n: Fresh Breeze Solo with Simonini Solo 210cc\nIncident Details\nOctober 9, 2022 10:00 AM Location of the incident:\nFort McMurray, Alberta, Canada\nType of Incident\n: Engine Malfunction\nI purchased this Fresh Breeze Solo 210cc second hand and the seller dismantled the engine before shipment. Upon delivery, in my garage I proceeded to mount the various parts \u2013 the carburetor, intake, exhaust and fuel tank \u2013 to the engine which was already mounted to the frame, then completed the assembly by adding the cage to the frame. I inspected everything including the carburetor piston and all appeared to be in very good condition. So, I decided to take it out into the driveway, start it up and see if it would run as well as it did in the video the seller sent me.\nNow, a little bit of background. I started flying fixed wing airplanes in 1967, obtaining my PPL in 1968. I first saw paragliding while in Cape Town, South Africa, in early 1991 and realized it would be a great way to combine flying with my travels. But, it wasn\u2019t until 1997 when living on Phuket in southern Thailand that I finally got around to buying one, in fact two \u2013 Fly Constelluccio Solo and Tandem units from Italy. Being the only person with a paramotor in southern Thailand at that time, I had to teach myself to fly and did so successfully, flying regularly between 1997 and 2003 when I sold the units before departing Uganda where I had been living for two years.\nThe fact is I often did ground starts on my Fly Constelluccio units and never once experienced any difficulties. So, you could quite rightly deduce that I was completely unaware of any potential for injury. In all my years of powered paragliding, I was always alone. I never met another paramotor pilot nor came across any sources of paragliding information on my limited internet access in Asia and Africa. In the intervening years, my attention turned to microlights with the purchase of a Polaris Flying Inflatable Boat. So, until last year when I saw an ad for a used paramotor, I hadn\u2019t really thought too much about the sport.\nWhen the paramotor arrived, it didn\u2019t occur to me that there was a considerable difference in power between this Solo 210cc and my smaller Fly units. So, I started it up in the driveway that morning just as I had done countless times with my original paramotors two decades ago.\nUnfortunately, something unforeseen caused the engine to immediately accelerate to full throttle, causing the paramotor to pitch forward and tumble towards me. The propeller blade first hit my chest on my left side at heart level and I presume I must have pushed out with my arms to try to deflect the propeller. Two extremely hard thumps on my right arm immediately informed me I had not been entirely successful in doing so.\nAfter striking me, the paramotor with its now broken propeller, lay flat on my lawn, the engine screaming at the highest revs. I managed to kill the engine, then picked up my arm parts and went into the house to find something to staunch the firehose like stream of blood gushing out from the severed arteries in what remained of my arm. In the house, I asked my autistic son to call 911 while I grabbed a roll of paper towels and pressed it firmly against my stump.\nMy son valiantly dialed 911 and although not a good communicator managed to summon the paramedics, making only a minor error in giving them the wrong address which I managed to correct in time, and in about seven minutes emergency services were at the door. I was told later that it only takes about seven minutes to ex-sanguinate when both arteries have been severed so, obviously, the roll of paper towels made a significant contribution to my survival.\nAt the small regional hospital that relies on a rotation system of doctors that work a circuit, I was fortunate the particular doctor on rotation that week had microsurgery skills and was able to re-establish blood flow to my hand. I was then put in an air ambulance and flown 400 miles south to the nearest major hospital. There, surgeons decided that they could not only reattach the lower part of my forearm, but replace the missing upper part with a section of the fibula and adjoining muscle and flesh from my right leg.\nAfter two months in the hospital, I finally returned home on October 9th, 2022 and have been undergoing regular occupational therapy ever since. At the moment, a fourth operation has been scheduled for later this month, September 20th, 2023, in which surgeons are going to create new extensor tendons from fibrous material in my hip so that I will once again be able to raise my hand at the wrist and spread my fingers.\nI plan on flying again. In fact, I have recently completed construction of a trike on which I have mounted my Fresh Breeze paramotor. The obvious take away from this incident is to avoid ground starts as much as possible especially with no-clutched propellers. And if for some reason a ground start is necessary, ensure the paramotor is firmly affixed to an immovable structure and your finger is touching the kill switch to instantly shut down the engine if need be.\nFlight Window\n: Morning\nWind Speed\n: N/A or Unknown\nType\n: N/A or Unknown\nPhase of Flight\n: Other/Unknown\nType of Injury\n: Major Injury\nCollateral Damage\n: None\nAnalysis of the incident (additional input by the incident investigation team)\n:\nPhotos\n(if available): https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6MTEzOTR8ZmlsZW5hbWU6MDgzQUIyRTgtMzI5OC00NUIyLUEzNjgtQTNBMjczNjZGMEE5XzFfMTA1X2MuanBlZ3xzaXplOnRodW1ibmFpbA==, https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6MTEzOTV8ZmlsZW5hbWU6M0Y3NTQ4NDktNDdCRC00QkExLUJEMEYtRENENTg1REYxMUE0XzFfMTA1X2MuanBlZ3xzaXplOnRodW1ibmFpbA==, https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6MTEzOTZ8ZmlsZW5hbWU6QzI1ODc5NUYtQTA0My00RkU1LTlGNkItRUYzMkRBRDRDOTUxXzFfMTA1X2MuanBlZ3xzaXplOnRodW1ibmFpbA==, https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6MTEzOTd8ZmlsZW5hbWU6QkMzMjY1MDgtMEMyOS00MTM0LTlBQzgtQjkyNkYxMTM4OTIwXzFfMTA1X2MuanBlZ3xzaXplOnRodW1ibmFpbA==, https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6MTEzOTh8ZmlsZW5hbWU6N0U4NTk2QkYtMTVCMS00N0VELUE1OTAtN0YzNjYyMDREOTg1XzFfMTA1X2MuanBlZ3xzaXplOnRodW1ibmFpbA==, https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6MTEzOTl8ZmlsZW5hbWU6OTI1QzFENzktRTNERi00NTFCLThEREMtMDE3NDNBQkIzQkVEXzFfMTA1X2MuanBlZ3xzaXplOnRodW1ibmFpbA==, https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6MTE0MDB8ZmlsZW5hbWU6SHVnaC1hbmQtaGlzLVRyaWtlLmpwZ3xzaXplOnRodW1ibmFpbA==\nVideo\n(if available):\nOther Files\n(if available):\nReturn to the incident list:\nIncident List",

When the prop hit the airbox, I heard a loud pop, the motor noise changed pitch, and I felt the entire motor torque. The airbox fell into an unpopulated, heavily wooded area. The motor was still running, the propeller did not cause any vibrations from the damage, the propeller still had normal thrust. I made the decision to kill the motor, land in an empty soccer field, & inspect the damage.\nWhy did this happen: This happened because the hose clamp was loose & the airbox was not tethered to the frame. This should have been caught during preflight or if I regularly checked the hose clamp. I did preflight the airbox before the flight & before every flight, but I was not doing an adequate job.\nFlight Window\n: Morning\nWind Speed\n: Light Wind (1-5 mph)\nType\n: Steady / Laminar\nPhase of Flight\n: Cruise/In Flight\nType of Injury\n: No Injury\nCollateral Damage\n: None\nAnalysis of the incident (additional input by the incident investigation team)\n:\nPhotos\n(if available): https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6MTEzNTh8ZmlsZW5hbWU6SU1HXzE5NjUuanBlZ3xzaXplOnRodW1ibmFpbA==, https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6MTEzNTl8ZmlsZW5hbWU6NjI0MDcxNTY5MzBfXzg2ODlFMjJGLUI3RjEtNEFBQy04MjJELTRDNjU0NjEwODcxQS5qcGVnfHNpemU6dGh1bWJuYWls\nVideo\n(if available):\nOther Files\n(if available):\nReturn to the incident list:\nIncident List",

    "Raw_Text": "Return to the incident list:\nIncident List\nWrapped Tip Steer pushed Pilot toward Trees, Vehicles\nPPG Type\n: Foot Launch \u2013 Single Occupant\nType of Injury\n: Minor Injury\nPilot Details\nAge\n: 49\nWeight\n: 260\nGender\n: Male\nHighest rating held at the time of the incident\n: Advanced \u2013 PPG 3\nPilot experience level\n: 50+ hours\nGear Details\nWing Brand\n: MacPara\nModel\n: Charger\nSize\n: 31\nParamotor Frame\n: P2F Titan XL with Polini Thor 202\nIncident Details\nJune 25, 2023 7:45 AM Location of the incident:\nKill Devil Hills, North Carolina\nType of Incident\n: Hard Landing\nWing was set up at First Flight Airport in Kill Devil Hills, NC on the aircraft parking area. Many cracks in the surface had small, resilient weeds growing through them, creating line snag potential. On first takeoff attempt, it is believed that one or more lines were snagged and prevented the wing from coming up well. Pilot set the wing down and reset in the grass safety area to the west, aiming for a generally south to southwest takeoff heading, into the wind.\nWhile the pilot believes they cleared all of their lines while setting up the wing, it is believed that while moving the wing or after clearing the lines that the split tip-steer lines of the charger went through the riser.\nWhen the pilot clipped in, they did not notice that the tip steer line on the left side went through the riser. The brake lines were clear to the pulleys.\nThe pilot was able to kite and successfully take off, but immediately drifted to the left toward vehicles and the airport entry road.\nAt the time the winds were squirrely and the pilot believed it was a weird cross wind. They attempted to gain altitude and aim to the west, but continued to drift southeast toward the road.\nAfter realizing that going full throttle would not get over the trees, the pilot managed to do a hard landing on the tree-lined roadway, between two parked vehicles, with no damage to any 3rd party property.\nThe frame was gently scraped, one prop tip made contact with the pavement but can be easily sanded, and the pilots knees were scraped. No medical attention, beyond cleaning and bandaging, was necessary.\nThe pilot had 197 flights and 211 hours in flight over about 4 years of experience at the time of the incident. This is the worst injury the pilot had experienced.\nFlight Window\n: Morning\nWind Speed\n: Moderate Wind (5-9 mph)\nType\n: Gusty / Thermal Activity / Dust Devil\nPhase of Flight\n: Takeoff\nType of Injury\n: Minor Injury\nCollateral Damage\n: None\nAnalysis of the incident (additional input by the incident investigation team)\n:\nPhotos\n(if available): https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6MTA4NDl8ZmlsZW5hbWU6SU1HXzY2NjAtY29weS5qcGd8c2l6ZTp0aHVtYm5haWw=\nVideo\n(if available):\nOther Files\n(if available):\nReturn to the incident list:\nIncident List",

    "Raw_Text": "Return to the incident list:\nIncident List\nUn forecast gust front resulted in wing collapse and pilot fatality.\nPPG Type\n: Foot Launch \u2013 Single Occupant\nType of Injury\n: Fatality\nPilot Details\nAge\n: 65\nWeight\n:\nGender\n: Male\nHighest rating held at the time of the incident\n: Advanced \u2013 PPG 3\nPilot experience level\n: 50+ hours\nGear Details\nWing Brand\n:\nModel\n:\nSize\n:\nParamotor Frame\n:   with Vittorazi Atom 80\nIncident Details\nSeptember 3, 2022 8:00 AM Location of the incident:\nAlbuquerque, New Mexico\nType of Incident\n: Gust Front\nIncident Scenario\nIncident pilot departed his home field (Paramotor City near Albuquerque NM) at approximately 7:30 AM on September 3rd 2022 in his foot launched Powered Paraglider (PPG). Winds were light out of the North/Northeast and forecast to remain flyable for several hours.\nTwo ground witnesses observed a normal takeoff. Witness #1 then departed the field in his vehicle due to a mechanical issue with his PPG. Witness #2, who lives at the field, went back inside. No other pilots were flying that morning.\nApproximately 30-60 minutes later Witness #2 noticed the winds started gusting well in excess of 20 mph.\nWhen the incident pilot did not return to the flying field, witness #2 got in his truck to search for him.\nWitness #2 found the incident pilot deceased several miles north of the field. The PPG cage was extensively damaged and the wing wrapped around the cage and prop.\u00a0 Two impact marks were noted approximately 50 ft apart coming from a southeast direction with no drag marks in between.\nInvestigation\nWeather\nA review of historical weather data showed gusty winds were not forecast for that morning. It is unknown if the incident pilot had obtained any type of weather briefing prior to the incident flight.\nThe incident pilot departed the field at approximately 7:30 AM. Winds were light out of the North/Northeast and forecast to remain flyable for several hours.\nApproximately 30 minutes prior to the estimated takeoff time, an airport 18 miles ESE of the flying field (KABQ) began reporting winds gusting to 18 kts. At the estimated takeoff time they were reported to be gusting to 30 kts. Maximum winds that morning were reported as 26 kts steady with gusts as high as 35 kts. An AWOS Observation at an airport 11 miles NE of the field (KAEG) showed winds calm at the estimated takeoff time and gusting to 11 kts one hour later.\u00a0 Later observations showed winds of 17 kts steady with gusts to 21 kts at 9:47 AM local. It should be noted observations at this site are only taken once per hour at the estimated takeoff time. The Terminal Area Forecast (TAF) was updated at 7:37 AM due to the un-forecast wind gusts. Both observation sites showed winds decreasing later in the morning.\nBecause of the unusual un-forecast winds, the investigator requested help from the local National Weather Service (NWS) office to review the weather that day. (See attached report)\nInvestigation by the NWS revealed a rare, little known weather event had occurred. Overnight a group of thunderstorms had developed and dissipated approximately 180 miles east of the flying field. As is typical, a gust front developed from the dissipating storms. These gust fronts normally extend for 10\u2019s of miles before dying out. In this case however the gust front, aided by the prevailing winds out of the east, traveled over 180 miles to the incident location. A review of weather data from a reporting station 72 miles east of the flying field (KCQC) showed the wind gusting to 20 kts at 6:30 AM. Another station 53 miles east (K0E0) showed winds gusting to 22 kts at 7:35 AM.\nThe exact mechanism for these long traveling gust fronts is not known but they do appear to be sustained by prevailing winds. One other known example of this type of weather event was reported to have occurred at the Great Plains Balloon Race of 1997.\nTerrain\n30 miles east of the flying field is a canyon (Tijeras Canyon) flanked by mountain ranges extending to the north and south. This gap in the mountains is known for generating high wind events. When there is a high-pressure area east of the canyon, it acts as a venturi, accelerating the winds through the canyon and into the city.\nWhen the thunderstorm generated winds hit this canyon on the incident morning the already strong winds were accelerated with reported gusts as high as 35 kts at KABQ.\nAdditionally, the flying site is located in a shallow north-south oriented valley, several miles wide with a steep 600-foot escarpment of rough terrain on the east side. The incident pilot was found towards the east side of the valley. It\u2019s likely a westward moving gust front would result in increased turbulence downwind of the escarpment where the incident pilot was flying.\nPilot Experience\nIncident pilot earned his PPG2 wheel launch rating along with his PPG3 foot launch rating in April of 2015. Total flight time is unknown but he was highly regarded as one of the more experienced pilots at the field.\nMedical Conditions\nThe Office of the Medical Examiner did not respond to the investigator\u2019s request for an autopsy report. Witness reports suggest both legs were broken by impact.\nEquipment\nThe investigator was not granted access to the equipment or any inflight tracking instruments that may have been available. (The deceased was not known to carry any data trackers.)\nLimited pictures of the equipment in the back of a truck were reviewed but no conclusions could be drawn from them. From the pictures it was determined the paramotor was a Vittorazi Atom 80\nIt was reported that the incident pilot did not fly with a reserve parachute because he said he typically flew too low for it to be useful.\nIf altitude and time permit, a drag type reserve parachute (for example, G-lite, Shine, Mayday, Diamond Cross, Yeti UL or Fluid to name a few) may mitigate injury in similar situations. While they can still suffer from oscillations in turbulent conditions, they are less likely to collapse and tangle with themselves and equipment than gliding type of reserve parachutes.\nDetermination\nIt was determined that the incident pilot was caught in a strong, un-forecast wind event which led to a collapse of his canopy and fatal impact with the ground. Local terrain may have exacerbated the wind event.\nRecommendations\nIf conditions suggest a thunderstorm induced gust front as described in this report is possible, pilots should make a final wind check from observation sites far upwind of their location prior to takeoff.\nPilots should be aware of local terrain and how it may influence gusty, turbulent air\nThe USPPA instructional syllabus should include information on conditions that could generate these long duration gust fronts and weather analysis strategies which could detect them, such as those recommended in the attached NWS briefing.\nPilots should examine their preferred flying conditions and technique and determine if the addition of a reserve parachute could mitigate unforeseen conditions that might develop.\nRichard Greenwood\nAcme Rigging\nFlight Window\n: Morning\nWind Speed\n: Very Strong Wind (15+ mph)\nType\n: Gust Front\nPhase of Flight\n: Cruise/In Flight\nType of Injury\n: Fatality\nCollateral Damage\n: None\nAnalysis of the incident (additional input by the incident investigation team)\n:\nPhotos\n(if available):\nVideo\n(if available):\nOther Files\n(if available): https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6MTAxNDV8ZmlsZW5hbWU6V3hTdXBwb3J0Rm9yUGFyYWdsaWRlcnMucGRm\nReturn to the incident list:\nIncident List",
    "URL": "https://usppa.org/incidents/entry/1983"

    "Raw_Text": "Return to the incident list:\nIncident List\nMidair collision\nPPG Type\n: Foot Launch \u2013 Single Occupant\nType of Injury\n: No Injury\nPilot Details\nAge\n:\nWeight\n:\nGender\n: Male\nHighest rating held at the time of the incident\n: Unknown\nPilot experience level\n: 50+ hours\nGear Details\nWing Brand\n: Bdg\nModel\n: Unknown\nSize\n: Unknown\nParamotor Frame\n: Unknown Unknown with Unknown\nIncident Details\nFebruary 17, 2023 5:30 PM Location of the incident:\nMaricopa county, Arizona\nType of Incident\n: Mid-Air Collision, Pilot Error, See report\nIncident involved two pilots. Pilot A is referred to as experienced pilot with 50+ flight hours, pilot B is referred to as inexperienced pilot with less than 2 hours flight time.\nincident took place at approximately 5:30pm on Feb 17 2023 at flying circus fly in event. Pilot A (experienced pilot) was attempting a \u201cwing tip touch\u201d with pilot B (inexperienced pilot). Pilot A was on left side of pilot B and moving faster than pilot B. Video shows pilot A initiated right input on brake and right weight shift while at the same time passing pilot B whom was on pilot A right side. During this action and just prior to impact pilot B reduced throttle and decended approximately 20 feet. Pilot A just after right input which put pilot A in front of and slightly higher than pilot B let off throttle which cussed pilot B to impact pilot A from behind. Impact was pilot B leading edge of wing to pilot A cage and prop.\nvideo does not show clearly what lines tangled into pilot A prop and cage, however from post interview pilot B suggested it was their A lines. Video suggests this to be true as pilot B took approximately 30% right wing collapse which reinflated as both parties were descending.\nwhich both pilot A (now in front and on top) and pilot B in back and low where tangled, pilot A motor was running and their prop spinning and cutting pilot B leading edge wing, they both rode to the ground. Altitude is unknown but impact to ground was approximately 15 to 20 seconds. Pilot A did not kill engine at impact and rode running engine to ground where he killed engine after impact. Later reports suggest pilot A was flying with broken kill switch which was resets on given in post investigation as to why engine was not shut down. Further investigations suggest the broken kill switch was an issue for approximately the past year.\nboth pilots rode the collision to the ground with pilot B impacting first and pilot A impacting second. No injuries were recorded. Damage included broken prop (pilot A) and damaged wing (pilot B, unknown if pilot b sustained intact prop).\nFlight Window\n: Evening\nWind Speed\n: Moderate Wind (5-9 mph)\nType\n: Light and Variable\nPhase of Flight\n: Cruise/In Flight\nType of Injury\n: No Injury\nCollateral Damage\n: Other Pilot(s)\nAnalysis of the incident (additional input by the incident investigation team)\n:\nAfter review of video from Pilot A 360 camera it appears a wing tip touch was attempted. Pilot A was flying at a faster rate and forced wing tip touch with right brake input and weight shift while pilot B was attempting to break off contact with a descending input. During this pilot A stopped throttle input cussing pilot B to collide with pilot A. It should be noted pilot A give no \u201cout\u201d to pilot B to avoided collision.\nPilot A attempted wing tip touch with a very new pilot (pilot B).\nPilot A did not have a stable wing and did not match speed of pilot B.\nPilot A turned into and went to idle leaving pilot B no avenue to escape.\nPilot B was a new pilot, recommendation to not fly at large fly in until more air time is under the belt.\nWe can learn from this as wing tip touches are fun, they carry a responsibility of risk vs reward. Stable flight, known air condition, and pre planning should have been done. New pilots should never attempt this type of activity and experienced pilots should not entertain such actively with a new pilot.\nIt is all our responsibility to ensure proper function of our gear, this includes a working kill switch as, in this event, total wing failure due to in going prop to wing damage OR PROP to body contact could have occurred resulting in a fall to the ground or severe body injury.\nVideo of this incident is east to find, no link will be given.\nPhotos\n(if available):\nVideo\n(if available):\nOther Files\n(if available):\nReturn to the incident list:\nIncident List",

Pilot visually checked the wing, after inflation, but failed to notice the tumbleweed. Pilot went to full power and launched with the tumbleweed in the brakes, causing the glider to enter a left turn toward a tree. Luckily with full throttle the glider was able to climb enough to avoid a collision. A quick tug on the left brake shortly after missing the tree, cleared the tumbleweed from the lines.\nLaunch site has many tumbleweeds and getting rid of all of them is challenging. A more thorough visual inspection of the wing overhead, and laying out the wing further down the field to give more space to taxi, could have prevented this close call.\nFlight Window\n: Evening\nWind Speed\n: Light Wind (1-5 mph)\nType\n: Steady / Laminar\nPhase of Flight\n: Takeoff\nType of Injury\n: No Injury\nCollateral Damage\n: None\nAnalysis of the incident (additional input by the incident investigation team)\n:\nPhotos\n(if available):\nVideo\n(if available):\nOther Files\n(if available):\nReturn to the incident list:\nIncident List",

low light conditions near sunset, lack of lighting on the helicopter, lack of ADSB from the helicopter (at least I didn\u2019t see him on radar before I took off 15 minutes earlier \u2013 probably due to his low altitude), and his helicopter being of dark color against the dark trees while I was at a higher altitude than he was while approaching.\u00a0 My (Hunter) strobe was on.\u00a0 My wing is yellow/orange so very visible.\u00a0 The helicopter pilot evidently saw me before I saw him and increased his altitude while shifting away.\u00a0 He appears to pass me approximately slightly higher and 500-700 feet distant horizontally.\u00a0 Wind was nil, so he was not upwind of me while passing.\u00a0 I chose to turn 90 degrees away from his flight path and ascend to avoid potential rotor.\u00a0 I encountered no rotor from his aircraft as I departed.\u00a0 Other notes: I made radio calls before taking off, but had been silent since announcing that I was departing the pattern to the South after my launch.\u00a0 I had heard the helicopter pilot on the air band before take-off, but was unaware of his position or direction of travel (I did check FlightRadar before launching but he didn\u2019t show up.)\u00a0 Other than having ADSB-out, I\u2019m not sure I could have done anything else.\u00a0 I was doing wing-overs when (I assume) he saw me, so\u00a0 I couldn\u2019t have been any more obvious.\u00a0 Video of the incident is captured here.\u00a0 https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vtA-JtxQx7A\nFlight Window\n: Evening\nWind Speed\n: Light Wind (1-5 mph)\nType\n: Light and Variable\nPhase of Flight\n: Cruise/In Flight\nType of Injury\n: No Injury\nCollateral Damage\n: None\nAnalysis of the incident (additional input by the incident investigation team)\n:\nPhotos\n(if available):\nVideo\n(if available): https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vtA-JtxQx7A\nOther Files\n(if available):\nReturn to the incident list:\nIncident List",

Single Occupant\nType of Injury\n: Fatality\nPilot Details\nAge\n: 53\nWeight\n: 170\nGender\n: Male\nHighest rating held at the time of the incident\n: Unknown\nPilot experience level\n: 10-50 hours\nGear Details\nWing Brand\n: MacPara\nModel\n: Colorado\nSize\n: 23\nParamotor Frame\n:   with\nIncident Details\nJuly 3, 2020 7:00 PM Location of the incident:\nConroe, Texas\nType of Incident\n: Hard Landing\nRick was flying alone and there is no known video footage, so it should be understood that the exact nature and dynamics of Rick\u2019s crash are unknown.\u00a0 However, we do know much about the weather event/conditions that were involved with this fatality.\u00a0 This is because there were numerous PPG students and pilots in the air perhaps ~20 miles south of the accident location that experienced the same gust front event.\u00a0 From all accounts the day appeared to have \u201capparently\u201d nice wind conditions as evidenced by the (usppa rated) instructor that had several of his students flying at this same time.\u00a0 However, there clearly was a large cumulus development that collapsed and resulted in a significant outflow/gust-front event this particular evening.\u00a0 \u00a0 Our best guess is that once the gust front affected Rick\u2019s flying he attempted to land.\u00a0 Most likely that there was significant rotor present at his landing site which contributed to a fatal impact.\nThe accident occurred fairly close to where the cumulus collapse/outflow event originated, but the students that were ~20 miles away reported being caught in the gust front and having to attempt landings while flying backwards.\nJust wanted to get this into the database.\u00a0 Rick was member of our local informal flying group and will be missed.\u00a0 The learning here was better weather awareness, especially for the potential for large cumulus developments to collapse and result in significant outlfow/gust-front events (and to avoid flying in those conditions).\u00a0 Also, if caught in those conditions be aware of extreme rotor that comes with higher wind speeds (an LZ you thought was good might not be).\nFlight Window\n: Evening\nWind Speed\n: Frame\n: Yooda Yak with Moster 185\nIncident Details\nJune 24, 2022 6:39 PM Location of the incident:\nFarmersville, Texas\nType of Incident\n: Collision with terrain or ground obstacle\nBen Launched at 1830hrs, launching into the wind, made a huge 180 degree circle and headed across our north side\u00a0 tree line, on the other side Ben turned East, started to do some light ACRO, wing overs, nothing seemed wrong everything was pretty much normal, these trees are 50\u2032 tall, I would say he was no more than 100-120\u2032 in the air when he made another 180 Degree turn, getting more aggressive with his actions, his wing collapsed as he entered the second wing over, this was probably due to wind rotor since the wind was blowing North, hitting his wing as he flew west, Ben dropped behind the tree line and the motor could not be heard anymore.\nAs I approached the crash site I could tell that Ben had died, his legs were broken, you could tell he went head over heals and snapped his neck, after cutting him from the gear and wing lines we started CPR until the fire rescue showed.\nI believe in the last minute Ben tried to pull out of the drop by getting on this throttle, he hit with such force it burst his fuel tank along the bottom seam, it ripped the tank completely off, the top of his cage was bent backwards, from the forward roll, this snapped his neck, he died instantly.\nAfter I did CPR for some time I stepped back and started to piece things together, I know that he was at full throttle, he crashed in a corn field, when he hit the ground he slid 15 \u2013 20 yards.\nBen was a very experienced Pilot over 1200 flights, his flying became more aggressive and even closer to the ground, I was his Instructor and have warned him many times as his habits changed, I expressed my concerns over the low flying ACRO, I believe EGO is what killed my friend, he would often laugh at me and say yeah, yeah\u2026\nI believe he got so comfortable doing these stunts that he didn\u2019t expect this to happen and he forgot my MOST important rule\u2026 Leave yourself an out, had he been at 800\u2032 or more, we would not be going through this mental hardship.\nBen had been flying with another Pilot 8 days prior to this accident, this Pilot observed Ben flying low and doing wing overs, later after that flight Ben noted that he came very close to the ground and almost bragged about it, EGO and situational awareness is what brought my friend to his end.\nJust before Ben launched I fist bumped him for the last time, 9 minutes later\u2026. he was dead.\u00a0 This accident was witnessed by 3 Pilots, although we did not see the impact due to the trees\u2026 we saw the flight, wing overs and the collapse.\u00a0 \u00a0Our trees here are maybe 50-60 feet tall from our situation our vantage point I suspect Ben was no more than 150\u2032 in the air. this accident was avoidable. 100% Pilot error.\nI am having to deal with his family, I would     "Raw_Text": "Return to the incident list:\nIncident List\nDrag after landing in high winds\nPPG Type\n: Foot Launch\nType of Injury\n: No Injury\nPilot Details\nAge\n: 42\nWeight\n: 150\nGender\n: Male\nHighest rating held at the time of the incident\n: None\nPilot experience level\n: 50+ hours\nGear Details\nWing Brand\n: Ozone\nModel\n: Sirocco 3\nSize\n: 22\nParamotor Frame\n: Skymax Star with Vittorazi Atom 80\nIncident Details\nFebruary 4, 2022 8:30 AM Location of the incident:\nSalton Sea, California\nType of Incident\n: Fall or Trip (While on the ground), Ground Drag\nAt the 2022 Salton Sea fly-in a group of 4 pilots including myself planned to do a circumnavigation of the Salton Sea counterclockwise south to north.\u00a0 We planned to land at about the 3/4 point to refuel if needed.\u00a0 With favorable winds, I calculated I may not need to refuel.\u00a0 The wind at the LZ at sunrise was very light and the forecast on all Windy models was for it to remain that way until the afternoon.\u00a0 This forecast included the area on the other side (East) of the Salton Sea.\u00a0 After about 1/3 the way around the sea, I noticed the turbulence increasing (about 45min after sunrise) to moderate.\u00a0 As I made my turn around the southern tip of the Salton Sea, the turbulence increased and I suffered several small tip collapses.\u00a0 \u00a0At the 1/2 way point (directly East of the LZ across the sea) in the vicinity of Bombay Beach, the turbulence became severe.\u00a0 I tried several altitudes I settled on about 300AGL for the \u201cbest\u201d air.\u00a0 The wind had increased significantly from the East and my ground speed was now reduced to approx 10mph. I roughly calculated the wind speed to be about 15-18mph form the East.\u00a0 I figured the turbulence was coming from the mountain ridge to the east which was creating rotor.\u00a0 I considered climbing to get above the ridge,\u00a0 but given my 80cc motor, I figured it would take several long turbulent minutes to get above the ridge.\u00a0 My nerves got the best of me and I decided to stay where I was.\u00a0 \u00a0When I got to the Bombay Beach area, I decided to land and wait for the wind to die off.\u00a0 I picked this area since it was a little more populated in the event something went wrong on landing\u2026.I\u2019m glad I did.\u00a0 \u00a0As I touched down, my ground speed was almost 0.\u00a0 This meant the wind was approx 20mph sustained with gusts.\u00a0 I didn\u2019t have a solid plan once I touched down.\u00a0 This was my biggest mistake.\u00a0 About a second after touchdown, I was lifted off the beach about 1-2FT and twisted.\u00a0 I then fell to the ground and was being dragged towards the water.\u00a0 Pulling and wrapping brakes did nothing.\u00a0 I was able to get one riser unclipped but the wing did not deflate\u2026.I was confused until I realized I had speed bar connected.\u00a0 With gloves on, there was no way I was going to be able to get the S clips undone under pressure.\u00a0 I reached for my hook knife, but it wasn\u2019t there.\u00a0 I had forgotten to put it back on my harness after cleaning a few weeks back.\u00a0 \u00a0At this point I started to panic as I was still being pulled towards the water.\u00a0 \u00a0Then, out of nowhere, a man in a truck came speeding down the beach.\u00a0 He jumped out and collapsed the glider.\u00a0 I was about 20ft from the water at this point.\u00a0 In the end, this guy really saved my butt.\u00a0 I suffered no injuries, one hoop section was slightly bent.\u00a0 My glider suffered the most damage with several medium length tears and many small holes with an internal rib torn as well.\u00a0 Shout out to Cloud 9 for the fix on the glider!\u00a0 Major lessons learned:\u00a0 In areas with few weather sensors (east side of the Salton Sea, predictions may be wrong. 2. Have a plan for high wind landings.\u00a0 I should have grabbed the Cs or Bs.\u00a0 3. Fly with a hook knife.\u00a0 I was very lucky. Before the accident, I had been flying 3 years with over 400hrs of flight time.\nFlight Window\n: Morning\nWind Speed\n: Strong Wind (10-15 mph)\nType\n: Gusty / Thermal Activity / Dust Devil\nPhase of Flight\n: After Landing\nType of Injury\n: No Injury\nCollateral Damage\n: None\nAnalysis of the incident (additional input by the incident investigation team)\n:\nPhotos\n(if available): https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6ODM4OHxmaWxlbmFtZTpzczMuanBnfHNpemU6dGh1bWJuYWls, https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6ODM4OXxmaWxlbmFtZTpzczIuanBnfHNpemU6dGh1bWJuYWls, https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6ODM5MHxmaWxlbmFtZTpzczEuanBnfHNpemU6dGh1bWJuYWls\nVideo\n(if available):\nOther Files\n(if available):\nReturn to the incident list:\nIncident List",

I approached the dune on the leeward side and began to feel an increase in buffeting. There seemed to be a periodic phase to the turbulence as my right riser suddenly dropped about a foot, my machine listed starboard. At that time I realized I was entering an area of rotor and I initiated a quick turn to the left, hoping to exit the wind shadow. Unfortunately, the turn set me up for the next gust, five seconds later, which collapsed the right half of the wing. I struggled for support from the tight left riser, as the right riser collapsed, but the risers quickly twisted and spun me powerfully around two revolutions. Luckily, with half of the wing still flying, the rate of descent was controlled. From fifty feet, the fall was survivable and I suffered no injury. I failed to kill my engine before impact, so the prop was destroyed rolling around in the sand and a small piece of shrapnel tore a hole in the wing. It was a blessing being able to walk away from the crash.\nFlight Window\n: Evening\nWind Speed\n: Moderate Wind (5-9 mph)\nType\n: Steady / Laminar\nPhase of Flight\n: Cruise/In Flight\nType of Injury\n: No Injury\nCollateral Damage\n: None\nAnalysis of the incident (additional input by the incident investigation team)\n:\nPhotos\n(if available): https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6Nzk5MXxmaWxlbmFtZToxLmpwZ3xzaXplOnRodW1ibmFpbA==, https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6Nzk5MnxmaWxlbmFtZToyLmpwZ3xzaXplOnRodW1ibmFpbA==, https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6Nzk5M3xmaWxlbmFtZTozLmpwZ3xzaXplOnRodW1ibmFpbA==, https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6Nzk5NHxmaWxlbmFtZTo0LmpwZ3xzaXplOnRodW1ibmFpbA==, https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6Nzk5NXxmaWxlbmFtZTo1LmpwZ3xzaXplOnRodW1ibmFpbA==, https://usppa.org/frm_file/aWQ6Nzk5NnxmaWxlbmFtZTpwb3N0LWNyYXNoLmpwZWd8c2l6ZTp0aHVtYm5haWw=\nVideo\n(if available):\nOther Files\n(if available):\nReturn to the incident list:\nIncident List",

    "Raw_Text": "Return to the incident list:\nIncident List\nAccidental pressing of kill switch after takeoff causing landing in trees\nPPG Type\n:\nType of Injury\n: No Injury\nPilot Details\nAge\n: 54\nWeight\n: 190\nGender\n: Male\nHighest rating held at the time of the incident\n: Advanced \u2013 PPG 3\nPilot experience level\n: 50+ hours\nGear Details\nWing Brand\n: Dudek\nModel\n: Universal\nSize\n: 25m\nParamotor Frame\n: Nirvana F Light 200 with Nirvana\nIncident Details\nOctober 12, 2019 8:00 AM Location of the incident:\nAustin, Texas\nType of Incident\n: Collision with terrain or ground obstacle, Pilot Error, killswitch is very easy to press\nI was taking off and successfully left ground. At approximately 100 feet over ground I pushed up into my seat with my right hand but must have simultaneously flexed my left hand and pressed the start/kill switch .\u00a0 The engine has a latency after turning off that lasts just a few seconds but prevents immediate restarting. I had sunk enough that I was about to brush the tops of\u00a0 the trees off the end of the runway, so I was not able to restart the engine in time. I landed in the trees and was left hanging by my wing in the canopy of the trees. I was able to climb down and the engine sustained no damage nor did I.\u00a0 It took me 2 days to cut away enough of the tree canopy to retrieve my wing which was also not harmed.\u00a0 \u00a0The design of the start/kill switch that is flush with the handpiece is very easy to press by accident and is a design flaw in my opinion. I have since glued a washer on top of the handpiece\u00a0 to prevent this type of inadvertent pressing\u00a0 of the button and it seems to have helped. I\u00a0 have been flying this equipment without other problems since this accident 3 years ago.\nFlight Window\n: Morning\nWind Speed\n: Light Wind (1-5 mph)\nType\n: Light and Variable\nPhase of Flight\n: Takeoff\nType of Injury\n: No Injury\nCollateral Damage\n: Property (building, vehicle, etc)\nAnalysis of the incident (additional input by the incident investigation team)\n:\nPhotos\n(if available):\nVideo\n(if available):\nOther Files\n(if available):\nReturn to the incident list:\nIncident List",

    "Raw_Text": "Return to the incident list:\nIncident List\nHeel prop strike\nPPG Type\n:\nType of Injury\n:\nPilot Details\nAge\n:\nWeight\n: 160\nGender\n:\nHighest rating held at the time of the incident\n:\nPilot experience level\n:\nGear Details\nWing Brand\n:\nModel\n: BGD Luna 2 23m\nSize\n:\nParamotor Frame\n: Miniplane ABM Flex Cage with Atom 80  with\nIncident Details\nAugust 25, 2021  Location of the incident:\n,\nType of Incident\n:\nTakeoff was a forward launch on a very humid, hot, and calm evening down the runway, starting at the end of the runway. Approximately 30 feet down from the the point of inflation, there were some wide divots (~2-4 inches deep) in the grass.    Inflation was fine, however there was a minor oscillation. I believe this oscillation on the surge, along with the runway ruts and a fairly long inseam (leg length) led to a prop strike with my right heel as the wing lost some lift (lines did not slack). I immediately aborted, did not fall over, and the paramotor never impacted the ground.    One blade of the two blade carbon fiber Yueny propeller was destroyed on impact with my heel. Other than the propeller, there was minor damage to the netting from the propeller fragments.    The shoe I was wearing at the time of the incident, Allbirds, do not have a rigid heel, and have very little padding. I believe this contributed to the severity of the injury, and I believe a shoe with rigid heel protection would have mitigated, or even prevented, injury.    I\u2019ve done some research and found the following post of a Paramotor pilot that had a heel strike while wearing Oboz boots, and walked away without injury. https://www.reddit.com/r/woodworking/comments/k9udqg/while_flying_paramotor_i_had_a_prop_strike/\nFlight Window\n:\nWind Speed\n:\nType\n:\nPhase of Flight\n:\nType of Injury\n:\nCollateral Damage\n:\nAnalysis of the incident (additional input by the incident investigation team)\n:\nPhotos\n(if available):\nVideo\n(if available):\nOther Files\n(if available):\nReturn to the incident list:\nIncident List",

The safety cage had been removed earlier during disassembly of trike.  Engine was mounted to frame which was standing on garage floor.  The pilot was standing behind the engine and holding the frame with one arm.  Engine was started using the electric starter. I made a turn which resulted in a considerable amount of energy built up.  Instead of letting up on the power and controlling the energy after the turn, I held full power.  This resulted in me being at the same altitude as my wing, by which point I had realized my mistake.    I knew the next thing to happen was for me to fall down (backwards) while my wing rebuilt pressure.  As that happened, the wing must have loaded asymmetrically or I pulled on one break more than the other.  After the surge, my lines were twisted 180 and, as I hadn\u2019t taken off too long before, I was low and had very little time to resolve the issue.    I tried controlling the wing but unfortunately started turning and lost the little altitude that I had left and ended up on the ground.     "Raw_Text": "Return to the incident list:\nIncident List\nHard Landing\nPPG Type\n:\nType of Injury\n:\nPilot Details\nAge\n: 40\nWeight\n: 170\nGender\n:\nHighest rating held at the time of the incident\n:\nPilot experience level\n:\nGear Details\nWing Brand\n:\nModel\n: Skywalk Mescal Small\nSize\n:\nParamotor Frame\n: Thor130  with\nIncident Details\nJanuary 10, 2020  Location of the incident:\n,\nType of Incident\n:\nPilot was flying nil wind conditions late in the day.  Took off from large parking lot surrounded by playing fields. During flight a vehicle drove into the area and parked in center of parking lot which was the primary LZ. It is very unusual for this location to have activity late in the day and never in the center of the parking lot (i.e. people come for the fields/ playground and park on edge of the lot near the desired destination).  Presence of the vehicle resulted in useable LZ being approximately 60% smaller.    Pilot decided to land in the smaller space.  Due to reduced LZ area, pilot choose to kill motor at a lower altitude than normal to preserve optionality to throttle up and abort landing if approach did not look good.     Pilot killed motor and got out of harness at approximately 40\u2032 AGL. Immediately prior to landing, wing surged forward and accelerated pilot into the ground. In video review, wing surge happened less than 2 seconds before impact.  Pilot did not have time to check the surge. Landing seemed \u201cnormal\u201d up until final seconds of flight.    Pilot put legs out to brace the impact and rolled.  Crash resulted in a broken fibula, dislocated ankle and tendon damage. Pilot spent 3 days in the hospital and required a metal plate to repair broken fibula. PPG frame suffered heavy damage (zenith) and carbon fiber prop snapped.    In hindsight, wing surge was likely due to late cutting of motor combined with inadvertent pulling/ release of brakes caused by getting out of the harness. A big takeaway is that going from seated to landing position in the harness can easily result in pull/release on the brakes.  Not a problem if done at 100\u2032 but not good to have happen low.    Lessons learned from the accident include: 1) Have good boots and tie them tight. Pilot was wearing high top hiking boots but did not have them tied tightly. Better boots may have prevented the break/dislocation 2) Parking lots are very hard. This lot was compacted gravel and pilot has sworn to never land on anything other than grass or sand in the future 3) Be prepared for changing LZ conditions and practice the skill set to land in a smaller area 4) Have phone where it can be reached with one hand 5) Ambulances have pain meds on board. Once the shock wears off, the ride to the hospital can be very painful everytime your buddies car hits a pothole!\nFlight Window\n:\nWind Speed\n:\nType\n:\nPhase of Flight\n:\nType of Injury\n:\nCollateral Damage\n:\nAnalysis of the incident (additional input by the incident investigation team)\n:\nPhotos\n(if available):\nVideo\n(if available):\nOther Files\n(if available):\nReturn to the incident list:\nIncident List",

    "Raw_Text": "Return to the incident list:\nIncident List\nRecognizing and Avoiding Power Oscillations in Fli\nPPG Type\n:\nType of Injury\n:\nPilot Details\nAge\n: 54\nWeight\n: 160\nGender\n:\nHighest rating held at the time of the incident\n:\nPilot experience level\n:\nGear Details\nWing Brand\n:\nModel\n: Dudek Universal 1.1 25.5M\nSize\n:\nParamotor Frame\n: Scout Carbon  with\nIncident Details\nOctober 2, 2019  Location of the incident:\n,\nType of Incident\n:\nNot long out of training, I was flying in the evening at the regular field. Made a low pass over the LZ to check the wind direction before landing. Winds at the surface were approaching calm. The sun was pretty low and there was just a slight shear at 100 feet or so. This evening, that\u2019s where I was leveled off for the low approach. The wing would surge and lag slightly at that layer. Rather than (correctly) checking that with some brakes, I was adding throttle on the dives and chopping throttle on the climbs to stay level. It didn\u2019t take long for that to get completely out of control. Within what seems like about 3 or 4 oscillations, I was like a kid on swing, modulating power at all the wrong times, losing a little altitude every cycle. With continuous power I could have climbed out of this mess (and the shear), but I was too busy chasing the horizon going up and down. On the last swing forward I was feeling light in the lines (not good), made one more swing back and halfway forward again before hitting the ground. Luckily, my feet were up, so the frame took the impact instead of me. I was very lucky to hit the ground at the bottom of a swing and walk away with no injuries. Lessons learned: My PPG training covered oscillations, mostly for the side-to-side scenario on landings. I should have paid more attention in class to the ones that throttle (and brakes) can bring on. When my mishap started, I fixated on solving, with throttle, a problem that a little application of brakes would have handled nicely.\nFlight Window\n:\nWind Speed\n:\nType\n:\nPhase of Flight\n:\nType of Injury\n:\nCollateral Damage\n:\nAnalysis of the incident (additional input by the incident investigation team)\n:\nPhotos\n(if available):\nVideo\n(if available):\nOther Files\n(if available):\nReturn to the incident list:\nIncident List",
